stops.name: Piazza del Duomo
stops.shortDescription: The monumental central square of Milan, framed by some of the city's most important buildings. It is heavily trafficked by locals, tourists, and hundreds of pigeons.

stops.name: Duomo di Milano
stops.shortDescription: A Gothic architectural marvel that took nearly six centuries to complete. It is adorned with over 3,400 statues, 135 gargoyles, and visitors can walk on its spectacular roof.

stops.name: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II
stops.shortDescription: Italy's oldest active shopping gallery and a major landmark of Milan. Its stunning glass arcades and mosaic floor feature a bull; spinning your heel on its testicles is said to bring good luck.

stops.name: Teatro alla Scala
stops.shortDescription: Inaugurated in 1778, this is one of the most prestigious opera houses in the world. Its relatively austere exterior hides a lavish, red-and-gold, multi-tiered auditorium inside.

stops.name: Piazza dei Mercanti
stops.shortDescription: A charming, intimate square that served as the center of city life in the Middle Ages. Its well-preserved buildings transport you instantly back to 13th-century Milan.

stops.name: Sforzesco Castle
stops.shortDescription: A massive fortress built in the 15th century by Francesco Sforza, Duke of Milan. Today, its grand courtyards and halls house several museums, including Michelangelo's unfinished final sculpture, the Rondanini Pietà.

stops.name: Parco Sempione
stops.shortDescription: Milan's primary city park, established in 1888 just behind the castle. It features winding paths, a scenic central lake, and expansive, romantic English-style gardens.

stops.name: Arco della Pace
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent neoclassical triumphal arch originally commissioned by Napoleon. It stands dramatically at the northwestern edge of Parco Sempione, often glowing beautifully at sunset.

stops.name: Santa Maria delle Grazie
stops.shortDescription: A striking Renaissance church and Dominican convent. It is world-renowned for housing Leonardo da Vinci's monumental mural, 'The Last Supper', painted directly onto the refectory wall.

stops.name: San Maurizio al Monastero Maggiore
stops.shortDescription: Often dubbed the 'Sistine Chapel of Milan', this unassuming 16th-century church hides an absolutely breathtaking interior that is completely covered in vibrant Renaissance frescoes.

stops.name: Pinacoteca Ambrosiana
stops.shortDescription: A historic library and art gallery founded in 1618. It holds masterpieces by Caravaggio, Titian, and importantly, the famous Codex Atlanticus containing Leonardo da Vinci's personal sketches.

stops.name: Via Montenapoleone
stops.shortDescription: The elegant centerpiece of Milan's 'Quadrilatero della Moda' (Fashion Quadrilateral). It is lined with the glittering flagship boutiques of the world's most luxurious fashion houses.

stops.name: Brera District
stops.shortDescription: Milan's charming, bohemian neighborhood. Characterized by its narrow, cobbled pedestrian streets, it is famously filled with antique shops, cozy cafes, and street-side fortune tellers.

stops.name: Pinacoteca di Brera
stops.shortDescription: Housed in a grand palazzo alongside the Academy of Fine Arts, this is the main public gallery for paintings in Milan, containing an unparalleled collection of Italian masterpieces.
